meta.name: AWS Solutions Architect Associate
meta.description: 10 key AWS services and concepts for the SAA-C03 exam.

cards.definition: EC2 instances are grouped into families optimised for different workloads: General Purpose (T, M) for balanced CPU/RAM; Compute Optimised (C) for CPU-intensive tasks; Memory Optimised (R, X) for large in-memory datasets; Storage Optimised (I, D) for high I/O; and Accelerated Computing (P, G) for GPUs/ML. Choosing the right family is a core exam topic.
cards.hint: Match the first letter of the family to its strength: C = Compute, R = RAM, I = I/O.

cards.definition: S3 offers tiered storage to balance cost and retrieval speed: S3 Standard (frequently accessed), S3 Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves objects), S3 Standard-IA and One Zone-IA (infrequent access), S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval, Glacier Flexible Retrieval (minutes–hours), and Glacier Deep Archive (up to 12 hours). Lifecycle policies automate transitions between classes.
cards.hint: Think of a temperature scale: Standard is hot, Glacier Deep Archive is frozen.

cards.term: Amazon RDS Multi-AZ vs Read Replicas
cards.definition: Multi-AZ creates a synchronous standby in a second Availability Zone for high availability and automatic failover — it is not used for reads. Read Replicas are asynchronous copies used to offload read traffic and scale read performance; they can be in the same region, a different region, or promoted to standalone DBs. Multi-AZ = HA; Read Replicas = performance.
cards.hint: Multi-AZ: one active, one on standby. Read Replica: many copies, all readable.

cards.term: AWS VPC Subnets, Route Tables, and NAT Gateway
cards.definition: A VPC is divided into public and private subnets. A public subnet has a route to an Internet Gateway (IGW) in its route table, allowing internet access. A private subnet has no IGW route. To let resources in private subnets initiate outbound internet connections without being reachable inbound, you place a NAT Gateway in the public subnet and add a route to it in the private route table.
cards.hint: Public subnet → Internet Gateway. Private subnet → NAT Gateway → Internet Gateway.

cards.definition: AWS offers three load balancer types: Application Load Balancer (ALB) — Layer 7, routes by HTTP/HTTPS path, host, and headers; Network Load Balancer (NLB) — Layer 4, ultra-low latency, handles millions of TCP/UDP requests per second; and Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) — routes traffic through third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS). ALB is most common for web apps.
cards.hint: ALB = smart HTTP routing; NLB = blazing fast TCP; GWLB = third-party appliances.

cards.definition: An IAM Role is an identity with permissions that can be assumed by AWS services, users, or external accounts — it issues temporary credentials. A Policy is a JSON document that defines allowed/denied actions on resources, attached to roles, users, or groups. The principle of least privilege means granting only the minimum permissions needed. EC2 instance profiles attach a role to EC2 instances.
cards.hint: Never hardcode access keys in code — attach a Role to the resource instead.

cards.definition: Route 53 is AWS's DNS service with smart routing: Simple (one record), Weighted (split traffic by %), Latency-based (nearest region), Failover (active/passive HA), Geolocation (route by user country/continent), Geoproximity (route by geographic distance with bias), and Multi-Value Answer (return multiple IPs with health checks). Used heavily in multi-region architectures.
cards.hint: Weighted = A/B test. Latency = fastest. Failover = DR. Geolocation = compliance.

cards.definition: An ASG automatically launches and terminates EC2 instances to maintain a desired capacity and respond to demand. Key settings: Minimum, Desired, and Maximum capacity. Scaling policies: Target Tracking (keep a metric at a target), Step Scaling (scale by steps when thresholds breach), and Scheduled Scaling (scale at specific times). Works with ELB for health checks and traffic distribution.
cards.hint: Min/Desired/Max — set a floor, a goal, and a ceiling for your fleet size.

cards.definition: SQS (Simple Queue Service) is a pull-based message queue: producers send messages, consumers poll and process them at their own pace. Messages persist until consumed (up to 14 days). SNS (Simple Notification Service) is a push-based pub/sub service: a publisher sends one message to a Topic, which fans out to all subscribed endpoints (email, SQS, Lambda, HTTP) simultaneously. Fan-out pattern = SNS → multiple SQS queues.
cards.hint: SQS = one consumer pulls from a queue. SNS = many subscribers get pushed the message at once.

cards.definition: AWS and the customer share security responsibilities divided at the hypervisor layer. AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud: physical infrastructure, hardware, global network, and managed services. The customer is responsible for security IN the cloud: operating system patches, network configuration, IAM, data encryption, application security, and firewall rules. The dividing line shifts depending on the service type (IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS).
cards.hint: AWS owns the building; you own everything you put inside it.
